Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative clause; Subject (Ukut) + Verb (anam) + Object (mmi) + Locative phrase (ke akpan)
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Small, hidden issues can cause serious consequences if ignored.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain Rephrasi: A minor problem can become serious.
Usage Contexts: Family advice, politics, moral instruction, community leadership
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from traditional Efik observation that insects in the head cause severe irritation, symbolizing overlooked threats.
First Example Sentence: Afo ukut anam mmi ke akpan; kop idem ke ini.
Second Example Sentence: Ke ufọk obio, ukut anam mmi ke akpan ndinam usen.
Third Example Sentence: Enye ikọfiọk ke ukut anam mmi ke akpan.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative clause; Noun (Mmọn) + Modal verb (ekeme) + Infinitive (ndidọk) + Object (ufọk)
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Unity and cooperation build strong foundations.
Usage Contexts: Community meetings, church gatherings, teamwork discussions
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects communal house-building culture among Efik riverine settlements.
First Example Sentence: Anyịn fiọk ke mmọn ekeme ndidọk ufọk.
Second Example Sentence: Ke church, pastor ọkọfiọk mmọn ekeme ndidọk ufọk.
Third Example Sentence: Mmọn ekeme ndidọk ufọk nte anyịn anam.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Comparative clause; Subject (Ete) + Verb (anam) + Object (ukem) + Comparative phrase (nte eyen)
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in extended family structures and chieftaincy traditions.
First Example Sentence: Ke ufọk, ete anam ukem nte eyen.
Second Example Sentence: Governor fiọk ke ete anam ukem nte eyen.
Third Example Sentence: Ete anam ukem nte eyen ke obio emi.

Idiom (Efik): Ukpa ke idem enye ama akpa
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Possessive clause; Noun (Ukpa) + Locative phrase (ke idem enye) + Perfect verb (ama akpa)
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone is overwhelmed by internal guilt or stress.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Ukpa (burden) symbolizes invisible emotional weight in Efik oral poetry.
First Example Sentence: Ámi mfiọk ke ukpa ke idem enye ama akpa.
Second Example Sentence: Ke ini enye ama anam mme, ukpa ke idem enye ama akpa.
Third Example Sentence: Ukpa ke idem enye ama akpa nte enye idịkpa.

Idiom (Efik): Utọñ ke itie ama anam idem
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative clause; Noun (Utọñ) + Locative phrase + Perfect verb (ama anam) + Object (idem)
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Success in the right place builds confidence.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Linked to performance praise traditions in Efik storytelling.
First Example Sentence: Utọñ ke itie ama anam idem mi.
Second Example Sentence: Ke school, utọñ ke itie ama anam idem eyen.
Third Example Sentence: Enye fiọk ke utọñ ke itie ama anam idem.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Equative clause; Noun phrase + Copula (edi) + Predicate adjective
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Expresses communal egalitarian ethics in Efik society.
First Example Sentence: Idem ke idem edi mkpa ke ufọk.
Second Example Sentence: Teacher ọkọfiọk idem ke idem edi mkpa.
Third Example Sentence: Ke obio emi, idem ke idem edi mkpa.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative declarative clause; Noun (Akpa) + Negative marker (ikọ) + Verb phrase
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Arrogance does not improve one's character.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects Efik communal values opposing prideful behavior.
First Example Sentence: Akpa ikọ anam idem enye ke obio.
Second Example Sentence: Ámi fiọk ke akpa ikọ anam idem enye.
Third Example Sentence: Eyen emi, akpa ikọ anam idem enye.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Possessive clause; Subject + Verb (enyene) + Object phrase
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: The child has a brave and noble heart.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common in praise poetry for young achievers.
Second Example Sentence: Ke competition, eyen enyene akpan esịt.
Third Example Sentence: Mama fiọk ke eyen enyene akpan esịt.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative clause; Subject (Eno) + Locative phrase + Verb phrase
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Drawn from traditional belief in internal moral awareness.
First Example Sentence: Eno ke akpan okop idem fo.
Second Example Sentence: Ke ikpe emi, eno ke akpan okop idem.
Third Example Sentence: Enye fiọk ke eno ke akpan okop idem.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative declarative clause; Subject (Ufan) + Negative marker (ikọ) + Verb + Comparative phrase
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A true friend does not ignore another’s situation.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Highlights strong kinship and friendship norms in Efik communities.
First Example Sentence: Ufan ikọ okop nte enye ke mkpa.
Second Example Sentence: Ámi fiọk ke ufan ikọ okop nte enye.
Third Example Sentence: Ke ini mfiọk mme, ufan ikọ okop nte enye.
